claim: The encoding reflects a 15th-century European scribe transcribing a Syriac pharmaceutical source: kaph/qoph merger (velar/uvular confusion), pharyngeal drop (het/ayin absent), gallows as paragraph markers — all consistent with a non-Semitic copyist using a purpose-built script.
evidence_for: Author profile aligns with radiocarbon date (1404-1438) and north Italian provenance
Jewish physicians working with Syriac sources in 15th-century Italy is historically documented
Multiple independent structural features converge on one copyist profile
evidence_against: Copyist hypothesis is unfalsifiable in its general form
Each structural feature in isolation has other possible explanations
test: Qualitative historical plausibility check; no direct statistical test
tests_remaining: Qualitative historical plausibility check; no direct statistical test
reasoning: Synthetic claim combining H-BRADY-02, 03, 04. Evaluate last.
source: Brady Defibaugh (April 2026), A Syriac Pharmaceutical Dispensatory Encoded in the Voynich Manuscript (DANI pipeline v31)
